title: Paddy Malone In Australia
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Och! my nameן¿½s Pat Malone, and Iן¿½m from Tipperary.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sure, I donן¿½t know it now Iן¿½m so bothered, Ohone!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the gals that I danced with, light-hearted and airy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Itן¿½s scarcely theyן¿½d notice poor Paddy Malone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis twelve months or more since our ship she cast anchor
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In happy Australia, the Emigrantן¿½s home,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from that day to this thereן¿½s been nothing but canker,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And grafe and vexation for Paddy Malone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, Paddy Malone! Oh, Paddy, Ohone!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bad luck to the agent that coaxed ye to roam.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wid a man called a squatter I soon got a place, sure,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d a beard like a goat, and such whiskers, Ohone!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he saidן¿½as he peeped through the hair on his faituresן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That he liked the appearance of Paddy Malone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wid him I agreed to go up to his station,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saying abroad in the bush youן¿½ll find yourself at home.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I liked his proposal, and ן¿½out hesitation
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Signed my name wid a X that spelt Paddy Malone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, Paddy Malone, youן¿½re no scholard, Ohone!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sure, I made a cris-crass that spelt Paddy Malone.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A-herding my sheep in the bush, as they call itן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was no bush at all, but a mighty great wood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wid all the big trees that were small bushes one time,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A long time ago, faithֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  I ן¿½spose ן¿½fore the flood.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To find out this big bush one day I went further,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The trees grew so thick that I couldnן¿½t, Ohone!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I tried to go back then, but that I found harder,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bothered and lost was poor Paddy Malone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, Paddy Malone, through the bush he did roam
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What a Babe in the Wood was poor Paddy Malone.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I was soon overcome, sure, wid grafe and vexation,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And camped, you must know, by the side of a log;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I was found the next day by a man from the station,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For I coo-eyן¿½d and roared like a bull in a bog.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The man said to me, ן¿½Arrah, Pat! whereן¿½s the sheep now?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Says I, ן¿½I dunno! barring one here at home,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the master began and kicked up a big row too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And swore heן¿½d stop the wages of Paddy Malone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Arrah! Paddy Malone, youן¿½re no shepherd, Ohone!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll try you with bullocks now, Paddy Malone.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To see me dressed out with my team and my dray too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wid a whip like a flail and such gaiters, Ohone!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the bullocks, as they eyed me, they seemed for to say too,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You may do your best, Paddy, weן¿½re blest if we go.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Gee whoa! Redman! come hither, Damper!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hoot, Magpie! Gee, Blackbird! Come hither,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whalebone!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the brutes turned round sharp, and away they did scamper,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And heels over head turned poor Paddy Malone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, Paddy Malone! youן¿½ve seen some bulls at home,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the bulls of Australia cows Paddy Malone.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I was found the next day where the brutes they did throw me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By a man passing by, upon hearing me groan,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wiping the mud from my face that he knew me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Says he, ן¿½Your nameן¿½s Paddy?ן¿½ ן¿½Yes! Paddy Malone.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I thin says to him, ן¿½Youן¿½re an angel sent down, sure!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No, faith, but Iן¿½m not; but a friend of your own!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And by his persuasion, for home then I started,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And you now see before you poor Paddy Malone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Arrah, Paddy Malone! you are now safe at home.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bad luck to the agent that coaxed ye to roam.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton) delivers a powerful performance in "Paddy Malone In Australia"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
